topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias leaned back in his ergonomic chair, the synthetic leather squeaking under his weight. The quarterly reports, a mountain of glossy, meaningless data, seemed to mock him. He traced a finger across a particularly egregious line item detailing 'synergistic optimization.' It all felt hollow, a carefully constructed façade about to crumble. When the executive announcement finally landed—a sterile email detailing a merger—Elias didn't flinch. He simply closed the file, the sound sharp in the quiet office. He pushed his chair back, the scrape of metal against the floor a deliberate, low sound. He stood up, his movements slow and deliberate, gathering his things as if preparing to leave a poorly maintained theater.

[story 2]

"Another pivot," Sarah muttered, not looking up from her screen. The air in the open-plan office felt thick, heavy with unspoken transactions. She watched the senior management team gather in the conference room, their smiles too bright, their gestures too practiced. Sarah felt a cold settling in her stomach, a familiar tightening around her ribs. She picked up her lukewarm coffee mug, the ceramic cool against her palm. When the news filtered down—a complete divestiture—she took a slow sip, letting the bitterness coat her tongue. She didn't speak. Instead, she methodically began tidying her desk, aligning the pens, smoothing the papers, each small, precise action a silent commentary on the entire charade.

[story 3]

Mark stared out the panoramic window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent neon. He felt a profound sense of detachment, as if he were observing a poorly staged play from the wings. The whispers started circulating in the breakroom, hushed tones laced with a brittle excitement. When the official memo confirmed the sale, Mark simply turned away from the chatter. He walked to the water cooler, his jaw tight. He didn't ask questions; there was no point in engaging with the spectacle. He filled his bottle, the plastic cool and solid in his hand, and then walked out, the sound of his footsteps echoing with a strange, resolute finality.
